2. Set Order to 2
3. Enter coefficients: a=3, b=-12, c=9
4. Press [SOLVE]

desmos.instructions: Type the equation 3x^2 - 12x + 9 = 0 to see the vertical lines at solutions, or graph y = 3x^2 - 12x + 9 and find x-intercepts.

calculator.description: Verify the roots of the quadratic equation

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The particle is at rest at these two times.

Answer:
$$t = 1 \text{ second and } t = 3 \text{ seconds}$$

steps.title: Interpret the condition
steps.content:
The question asks for the times when the particle is at rest. Physically, a particle is at rest when its instantaneous velocity is zero.

On a displacement-time graph, the velocity corresponds to the **gradient** (slope) of the tangent to the curve. Therefore, we are looking for points on the graph where the gradient is zero (horizontal tangent).

steps.title: Identify graph features
steps.content:
Points with a gradient of zero occur at the stationary points (turning points) of the graph. By observing the shape of the provided graph, we can identify two such points:

1.  A local maximum (the peak where the curve stops rising and starts falling)
2.  A local minimum (the trough where the curve stops falling and starts rising)

steps.title: Find coordinates using GDC
steps.content: To find the exact time values for these maximum and minimum points, we can graph the displacement function $s(t) = t^3 - 6t^2 + 9t + 2$ on a graphing calculator and use the analysis tools to locate the extrema.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Graph menu
2. Enter y1 = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 2 and Draw
3. Select G-Solv (F5) > MAX (F2) to find the peak
4. Select G-Solv (F5) > MIN (F3) to find the valley

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=] and enter Y1 = X^3 - 6X^2 + 9X + 2
2. Press [GRAPH]
3. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) > 4:maximum
4. Move cursor left/right of the peak and press [ENTER] twice to set bounds, then [ENTER] to guess
5. Repeat using 3:minimum for the valley

desmos.instructions:
1. Type s(t) = t^3 - 6t^2 + 9t + 2
2. Click or tap directly on the peak and valley points to see their coordinates

calculator.description: Graph the function and find the local maximum and minimum.

steps.title: State the solution
steps.content:
From the graphical analysis (or by reading the grid clearly), the local maximum occurs at $t = 1$ and the local minimum occurs at $t = 3$. 

Therefore, the particle is at rest at these times:
$$t = 1 \text{ s and } t = 3 \text{ s}$$

steps.title: Algebraic verification (Markscheme)
steps.content:
Although the graphical method is sufficient, the markscheme often awards marks for the algebraic process. We can confirm our result by differentiating the displacement function to find velocity and setting it to zero:

$$v(t) = s'(t) = 3t^2 - 12t + 9$$

Setting $v(t) = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned} 3(t^2 - 4t + 3) &= 0 \\ 3(t-1)(t-3) &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

This confirms the solutions $t=1$ and $t=3$.

parts.content: Determine the displacement of the particle at the times when it is at rest.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to substitute $t=1$ and $t=3$ into $s(t)$. <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate displacement at $t=1$: $s(1) = 1^3 - 6(1)^2 + 9(1) + 2 = 6$ m <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate displacement at $t=3$: $s(3) = 3^3 - 6(3)^2 + 9(3) + 2 = 2$ m <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<examTip>Remember to state the units (meters) in your final answer</examTip>

steps.title: Understand the condition
steps.content:
To find the displacement when the particle is **at rest**, we first need to find the times when the velocity is zero.

Velocity is the derivative of displacement with respect to time:
$$v(t) = s'(t)$$

steps.title: Find the velocity function
steps.content:
We find $v(t)$ by differentiating the given displacement function $s(t) = t^3 - 6t^2 + 9t + 2$ using the power rule ($f(x)=x^n \Rightarrow f'(x)=nx^{n-1}$).

$$\begin{aligned} v(t) &= \frac{d}{dt}(t^3 - 6t^2 + 9t + 2) \\ v(t) &= 3t^2 - 12t + 9 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for time
steps.content:
Set the velocity to zero to find the times when the particle is at rest:

$$3t^2 - 12t + 9 = 0$$

Divide the entire equation by 3 to simplify:

$$t^2 - 4t + 3 = 0$$

Factor the quadratic equation:

$$(t-3)(t-1) = 0$$

Therefore, the particle is at rest at $$t=1$$ and $$t=3$$.

gdc.instructions: In Equation mode, select Polynomial, degree 2. Enter coefficients a=3, b=-12, c=9. Solve.

ti84.instructions: Press [Y=], enter Y1=3X^2-12X+9. Press [GRAPH]. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC), select 2:zero. Follow prompts for left/right bound.

desmos.instructions: Type 3x^2 - 12x + 9 = 0. Click the intersection points with the x-axis to see solutions.

calculator.description: Find roots of the velocity equation

steps.title: Calculate displacement at t=1
steps.content:
Now, substitute $$t=1$$ back into the original displacement function $s(t)$ to find the position.

$$\begin{aligned} s(1) &= (1)^3 - 6(1)^2 + 9(1) + 2 \\ &= 1 - 6 + 9 + 2 \\ &= 6 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate displacement at t=3
steps.content:
Substitute $$t=3$$ into the displacement function $s(t)$.

$$\begin{aligned} s(3) &= (3)^3 - 6(3)^2 + 9(3) + 2 \\ &= 27 - 54 + 27 + 2 \\ &= 2 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The particle is at rest at displacements of **6 m** and **2 m**.

From the graph provided, these correspond to the local maximum at $t=1$ and the local minimum at $t=3$.

steps.title: Interpret "at rest" graphically
steps.content:
A particle is "at rest" when its velocity is zero. On a displacement-time graph, velocity is represented by the gradient (slope) of the curve.

Therefore, the particle is at rest at the points where the graph has a horizontal tangent (gradient is zero). These correspond to the **local maximum** and **local minimum** points on the graph.

steps.title: Identify time values from graph
steps.content:
By observing the provided graph, we can locate the turning points:
- The local maximum (peak) occurs at $t = 1$.
- The local minimum (valley) occurs at $t = 3$.

These are the times when the particle is at rest.

steps.title: Calculate displacement at t = 1
steps.content:
To find the exact displacement, we substitute $t = 1$ into the given displacement function $s(t) = t^3 - 6t^2 + 9t + 2$.

$$\begin{aligned} s(1) &= (1)^3 - 6(1)^2 + 9(1) + 2 \\ &= 1 - 6 + 9 + 2 \\ &= 6 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate displacement at t = 3
steps.content:
Next, we substitute $t = 3$ into the function to find the displacement at the second resting point.

$$\begin{aligned} s(3) &= (3)^3 - 6(3)^2 + 9(3) + 2 \\ &= 27 - 54 + 27 + 2 \\ &= 2 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Interpret the condition
steps.content:
The particle is **at rest** when its velocity is zero ($v(t) = s'(t) = 0$). On the graph of the displacement function $s(t)$, the points where the velocity (gradient) is zero correspond to the **local maximum** and **local minimum**.

To find the displacement at these times, we need to find the coordinates of these turning points on the graph.

steps.title: Find the local maximum
steps.content:
Use the graph analysis tools to locate the local maximum. This corresponds to the first time the particle comes to rest.

The calculator identifies the maximum at the point $(1, 6)$.

This means at time $t=1$, the displacement $s(1) = 6$.

steps.content:
Use the graph analysis tools again to locate the local minimum. This corresponds to the second time the particle comes to rest.

The calculator identifies the minimum at the point $(3, 2)$.

This means at time $t=3$, the displacement $s(3) = 2$.

steps.title: State the displacements
steps.content:
The question asks for the displacement at these times. From the $y$-coordinates of our maximum and minimum points, the displacements are:

$6 \text{ m}$ and $2 \text{ m}$

parts.content: Find the total distance traveled by the particle in the first 4 seconds.
parts.markscheme:
- State displacement at $t=0$: $s(0) = 2$ <marks>A1</marks>

- State displacement at $t=1$: $s(1) = 6$ <marks>A1</marks>

- State displacement at $t=3$: $s(3) = 2$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate displacement at $t=4$: $s(4) = 4^3 - 6(4)^2 + 9(4) + 2 = 6$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate total distance traveled:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Distance} &= |s(1) - s(0)| + |s(3) - s(1)| + |s(4) - s(3)| \\
&= |6 - 2| + |2 - 6| + |6 - 2| \\
&= 4 + 4 + 4 \\
&= 12 \text{ meters}
\end{aligned}
$$
<marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

<examTip>Pay attention to the direction of movement when calculating total distance</examTip>

steps.title: Strategy: Total Distance vs Displacement
steps.content:
To find the **total distance traveled**, we cannot simply subtract the initial displacement from the final displacement. We must account for the particle changing direction.

Using the **Sum of Displacement Segments** approach, we will:
1. Find the times when the particle changes direction (turning points) where velocity is zero.
2. Calculate the displacement at the start, end, and these turning points.
3. Sum the absolute distances between these consecutive points.

steps.title: Find turning points
steps.content:
The turning points occur when the velocity is zero. We find the velocity function $$v(t)$$ by differentiating the displacement function $$s(t)$$ with respect to time.

Using the power rule $$f(x)=x^n \Rightarrow f'(x)=nx^{n-1}$$:

$$\begin{aligned} s(t) &= t^3 - 6t^2 + 9t + 2 \\ v(t) = s'(t) &= 3t^2 - 12t + 9 \end{aligned}$$

Set $$v(t) = 0$$ to find the times:
$$\begin{aligned} 3t^2 - 12t + 9 &= 0 \\ 3(t^2 - 4t + 3) &= 0 \\ 3(t-3)(t-1) &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

So, the particle turns at $$t=1$$ and $$t=3$$. This matches the local maximum and minimum shown in the graph.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Equation/Solver mode
2. Enter 3x^2 - 12x + 9 = 0
3. Solve for x

2. Press [GRAPH] to view
3. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) and select 2:zero
4. Follow prompts to find x-intercepts

2. Calculate s(0), s(1), s(3), s(4)

calculator.description: Evaluate s(t) at key points

steps.title: Sum the segment distances
steps.content:
Now we calculate the distance traveled in each segment by taking the absolute difference between consecutive displacements. 

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Distance} &= |s(1) - s(0)| + |s(3) - s(1)| + |s(4) - s(3)| \\ &= |6 - 2| + |2 - 6| + |6 - 2| \\ &= |4| + |-4| + |4| \\ &= 4 + 4 + 4 \\ &= 12 \text{ meters} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate abs(6-2) + abs(2-6) + abs(6-2)

calculator.description: Calculate sum of absolute differences

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: The total distance traveled by the particle in the first 4 seconds is **12 meters**.

steps.title: Identify the method
steps.content:
To find the **total distance** traveled, we need to sum up all movement regardless of direction. In calculus, total distance is defined as the definite integral of the **speed** (the absolute value of velocity) over the given time interval.

$$ \text{Distance} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} |v(t)| \, dt $$

We need to find the velocity function $v(t)$ and then evaluate the integral from $t=0$ to $t=4$.

steps.title: Find the velocity function
steps.content:
Velocity is the derivative of displacement with respect to time ($v(t) = s'(t)$). We apply the power rule from the data booklet to differentiate $s(t)$.

Given displacement:
$$ s(t) = t^3 - 6t^2 + 9t + 2 $$

Differentiating term by term:

$$\begin{aligned} v(t) &= \frac{d}{dt}(t^3) - \frac{d}{dt}(6t^2) + \frac{d}{dt}(9t) + \frac{d}{dt}(2) \\ v(t) &= 3t^2 - 12t + 9 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Set up the definite integral
steps.content:
Now we substitute our velocity function into the distance formula. The time interval is the first 4 seconds, so our limits are from $0$ to $4$.

$$ \text{Distance} = \int_{0}^{4} |3t^2 - 12t + 9| \, dt $$

The absolute value bars $|...|$ are critical because the particle changes direction. Without them, we would find the net displacement, not the total distance.

steps.title: Evaluate using a graphing calculator
steps.content: Use the numerical integration feature on your calculator to evaluate the integral directly. This handles the absolute value (changes in direction) automatically.

gdc.instructions:
In Run-Matrix mode, press OPTN > CALC > ∫dx.
Enter the expression with absolute value: ∫(|3x^2 - 12x + 9|, 0, 4)

ti84.instructions:
Press MATH > 9 (fnInt).
Enter the absolute value using MATH > NUM > 1 (abs).
Fill in the template: fnInt(abs(3X^2 - 12X + 9), X, 0, 4)

desmos.instructions:
Type 'int' to create an integral symbol.
Enter limits 0 and 4.
Type abs(3x^2 - 12x + 9) dx

calculator.description: Evaluate the definite integral of absolute velocity

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator yields a result of $12$. 

Therefore, the total distance traveled is **12 meters**.

*(Note: This matches the markscheme method of summing the distances between turning points: $|s(1)-s(0)| + |s(3)-s(1)| + |s(4)-s(3)| = 4 + 4 + 4 = 12$)*

questions.specification:
Two particles, P and Q , move along the same straight line. Particle P has velocity $v_{P}(t)=3 t-\frac{1}{2} t^{2}$, and particle Q has constant velocity $v_{Q}=4 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, for $t \geq 0$. Particle P is at the origin at $t=0$. All lengths are in metres and time is in seconds.

parts.content: Find the time $t$ when the distance between particles P and Q is at a minimum. [
parts.markscheme:
- Displacement of P: $s_{P}(t)=\int\left(3 t-\frac{1}{2} t^{2}\right) d t=\frac{3}{2} t^{2}-\frac{1}{6} t^{3}+c, c=0$ (at $t=0, s_{P}=0$ ) <marks>M1</marks>
- Displacement of Q: $s_{Q}(t)=4 t$

- Minimize by finding critical points: $\frac{d}{d t}\left(\frac{3}{2} t^{2}-\frac{1}{6} t^{3}-4 t\right)=3 t-\frac{1}{2} t^{2}-4=0$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Solving: $t^{2}-6 t+8=0, t=2,4$ seconds <marks>A1</marks>


Note: Award <marks>A1</marks> for either correct time, but both must be given for full marks.

questions.specification:
In this question, all lengths are in metres and time is in seconds.

Two particles, A and B, move along the same straight line. Particle A has velocity 
$$v_A(t) = 3t - t^2, \quad \text{for } 0 \leq t \leq 4$$

Particle B has displacement 
$$s_B(t) = 2t^2 - 8t, \quad \text{for } t \geq 0$$

Both particles start at the origin at $t = 0$.

parts.content: Find the times when particle A's acceleration is zero in $0 \leq t \leq 4$.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognizing acceleration is the derivative of velocity: $a_A(t) = \frac{d}{dt}v_A(t)$.
- $a_A(t) = \frac{d}{dt}(3t - t^2) = 3 - 2t$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Set acceleration to zero: $3 - 2t = 0$, so $t = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5$ seconds. <marks>A1</marks>
- Verification: $t = 1.5$ is within the domain $[0,4]$. <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Determine the times when particles A and B have the same velocity.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognizing velocity of B is the derivative of displacement: $v_B(t) = \frac{d}{dt}s_B(t)$.
- $v_B(t) = \frac{d}{dt}(2t^2 - 8t) = 4t - 8$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Set velocities equal: $3t - t^2 = 4t - 8$. <marks>A1</marks>
- Rearranging: $-t^2 - t + 8 = 0$ or $t^2 + t - 8 = 0$. <marks>A1</marks>
- Using quadratic formula: $t = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1 + 32}}{2} = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{33}}{2}$, giving $t \approx 2.37$ seconds (taking positive root only as $t \geq 0$). <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Find the time when the distance between particles A and B is maximized in $0 \leq t \leq 4$.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognizing displacement of A requires integration: $s_A(t) = \int_0^t v_A(\tau) d\tau$.
- Integrating: $s_A(t) = \int_0^t (3\tau - \tau^2) d\tau = \left[\frac{3\tau^2}{2} - \frac{\tau^3}{3}\right]_0^t = \frac{3t^2}{2} - \frac{t^3}{3}$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Distance function: $D(t) = |s_A(t) - s_B(t)| = \left|\frac{3t^2}{2} - \frac{t^3}{3} - (2t^2 - 8t)\right| = \left|-\frac{t^2}{2} - \frac{t^3}{3} + 8t\right|$. <marks>A1</marks>
- To find extrema, set $\frac{d}{dt}[s_A(t) - s_B(t)] = 0$: $v_A(t) - v_B(t) = 0$, which gives $t \approx 2.37$ from part (b). <marks>A1</marks>
- Evaluating distance at critical point and endpoints: $D(0) = 0$, $D(2.37) = \left|-\frac{(2.37)^2}{2} - \frac{(2.37)^3}{3} + 8(2.37)\right| \approx 9.46$, $D(4) = \left|-8 - \frac{64}{3} + 32\right| = \left|\frac{8}{3}\right| \approx 2.67$. <marks>A1</marks>
- Maximum distance occurs at $t \approx 2.37$ seconds with distance approximately $9.46$ metres. <marks>A1</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: This is a kinematics problem starting from a given acceleration a(t)=2t−1, integrating to find velocity and then displacement s(t), using displacement conditions at two times to determine constants.

parts.content: A particle moves along a horizontal path such that at time $t$ seconds, $t \geq 0$, its acceleration $a$ is given by $a = 2t - 1$. When $t = 6$, its displacement $s$ from a fixed point P is 18.25 m. When $t = 15$, its displacement from P is 922.75 m. Find an expression for $s$ in terms of $t$.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to integrate $a$ to find $v$ <marks>M1</marks>

- $v = \int a\,dt = \int (2t - 1) \,dt$

- State velocity function: $v = t^2 - t + c$ <marks>A1</marks>

- $s = \int v\,dt = \int (t^2 - t + c) \,dt$

- State general displacement function: $s = \frac{t^3}{3} - \frac{t^2}{2} + ct + d$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Attempt at substitution of given values <marks>M1</marks>

- At $t = 6$, $18.25 = 72 - 18 + 6c + d$

- At $t = 15$, $922.75 = 1125 - 112.5 + 15c + d$

- Solve simultaneously <marks>M1</marks>

- $c = -6, d = 0.25$

- State final expression: $s = \frac{t^3}{3} - \frac{t^2}{2} - 6t + \frac{1}{4}$ <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

steps.title: Recall kinematics relationships
steps.content:
To find the displacement $s$ from acceleration $a$, we must integrate acceleration twice. 

1. Integrating acceleration $a$ gives velocity $v$: 
   $$v = \int a \, dt$$

2. Integrating velocity $v$ gives displacement $s$:
   $$s = \int v \, dt$$

We will perform these integrations sequentially, adding a constant of integration each time ($c$ and $d$).

steps.title: Integrate acceleration to find velocity
steps.content:
First, we integrate the given acceleration function $a = 2t - 1$ with respect to $t$ to find the velocity $v$. Using the power rule for integration:

$$\int x^n \, dx = \frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}$$

$$\begin{aligned} v &= \int (2t - 1) \, dt \\ v &= 2\left(\frac{t^2}{2}\right) - 1(t) + c \\ v &= t^2 - t + c \end{aligned}$$

Here, $c$ is our first constant of integration representing the initial velocity.

steps.title: Integrate velocity to find displacement
steps.content:
Next, we integrate the velocity expression $v = t^2 - t + c$ to find the displacement $s$. We introduce a second constant of integration $d$:

$$\begin{aligned} s &= \int (t^2 - t + c) \, dt \\ s &= \frac{t^3}{3} - \frac{t^2}{2} + ct + d \end{aligned}$$

This is our general expression for displacement. We now have two unknown constants, $c$ and $d$.

steps.title: Apply first boundary condition
steps.content:
We use the information given in the question to find $c$ and $d$. 

When $t = 6$, displacement $s = 18.25$. Substituting these values into our general displacement equation:

$$\begin{aligned} 18.25 &= \frac{6^3}{3} - \frac{6^2}{2} + c(6) + d \\ 18.25 &= \frac{216}{3} - \frac{36}{2} + 6c + d \\ 18.25 &= 72 - 18 + 6c + d \\ 18.25 &= 54 + 6c + d \end{aligned}$$

Rearranging for the constants:

$$6c + d = -35.75 \quad \text{--- (Equation 1)}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value of the numeric terms

ti84.instructions: Calculate the value of the numeric terms

desmos.instructions: Calculate the value of the numeric terms

calculator.description: Calculate the constant terms for Equation 1

highlights.text: When $t = 6$, its displacement $s$ from a fixed point P is 18.25 m

steps.title: Apply second boundary condition
steps.content:
Now we use the second condition: when $t = 15$, displacement $s = 922.75$. Substituting these into the general equation:

$$\begin{aligned} 922.75 &= \frac{15^3}{3} - \frac{15^2}{2} + c(15) + d \\ 922.75 &= \frac{3375}{3} - \frac{225}{2} + 15c + d \\ 922.75 &= 1125 - 112.5 + 15c + d \\ 922.75 &= 1012.5 + 15c + d \end{aligned}$$

Rearranging for the constants:

$$15c + d = -89.75 \quad \text{--- (Equation 2)}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value of the numeric terms

ti84.instructions: Calculate the value of the numeric terms

desmos.instructions: Calculate the value of the numeric terms

calculator.description: Calculate the constant terms for Equation 2

highlights.text: When $t = 15$, its displacement from P is 922.75 m

steps.title: Solve simultaneous equations
steps.content:
We now have a system of two linear equations with two unknowns:

1. $6c + d = -35.75$
2. $15c + d = -89.75$

Subtract Equation 1 from Equation 2 to eliminate $d$:

$$\begin{aligned} (15c + d) - (6c + d) &= -89.75 - (-35.75) \\ 9c &= -54 \\ c &= -6 \end{aligned}$$

Substitute $c = -6$ back into Equation 1 to find $d$:

$$\begin{aligned} 6(-6) + d &= -35.75 \\ -36 + d &= -35.75 \\ d &= 0.25 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the difference in values by the difference in coefficients

ti84.instructions: Divide the difference in values by the difference in coefficients

desmos.instructions: Divide the difference in values by the difference in coefficients

calculator.description: Solve for c

steps.title: State final expression
steps.content:
Finally, we substitute the values of $c = -6$ and $d = 0.25$ (or $\frac{1}{4}$) back into our general displacement expression:

$$s = \frac{t^3}{3} - \frac{t^2}{2} - 6t + 0.25$$

Alternatively, using fractions:
$$s = \frac{t^3}{3} - \frac{t^2}{2} - 6t + \frac{1}{4}$$

steps.title: Find the velocity function
steps.content:
To find the displacement, we first need the velocity. We know that velocity is the integral of acceleration with respect to time.

Using the power rule for integration:

$$\int x^n \, dx = \frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1} + C$$

$$\begin{aligned} v &= \int a \, dt \\ &= \int (2t - 1) \, dt \\ &= t^2 - t + c \end{aligned}$$

Here, $c$ is the constant of integration representing the initial velocity or a constant shift.

steps.title: Set up definite integral for displacement
steps.content:
Instead of finding the general solution immediately, we can use the **Definite Integration Approach**. The definite integral of velocity from $t_1$ to $t_2$ equals the change in displacement between those times.

We are given $s(6) = 18.25$ and $s(15) = 922.75$.

$$\int_{6}^{15} v \, dt = s(15) - s(6)$$

Substituting our expression for $v$ and the given values:

$$\int_{6}^{15} (t^2 - t + c) \, dt = 922.75 - 18.25$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 922.75 - 18.25

ti84.instructions: Calculate 922.75 - 18.25

calculator.description: Calculate the change in displacement

highlights.text: When $t = 6$, its displacement $s$ from a fixed point P is 18.25 m
When $t = 15$, its displacement from P is 922.75 m

steps.title: Solve for the velocity constant c
steps.content:
Now we evaluate the integral to solve for $c$. We can split the integral into the variable part and the constant part:

$$\int_{6}^{15} (t^2 - t) \, dt + \int_{6}^{15} c \, dt = 904.5$$

Evaluating the first part:
$$\begin{aligned} \left[ \frac{t^3}{3} - \frac{t^2}{2} \right]_6^{15} &= \left(\frac{15^3}{3} - \frac{15^2}{2}\right) - \left(\frac{6^3}{3} - \frac{6^2}{2}\right) \\ &= (1125 - 112.5) - (72 - 18) \\ &= 1012.5 - 54 \\ &= 958.5 \end{aligned}$$

Evaluating the constant part:
$$\int_{6}^{15} c \, dt = c(15 - 6) = 9c$$

Putting it all together:
$$\begin{aligned} 958.5 + 9c &= 904.5 \\ 9c &= 904.5 - 958.5 \\ 9c &= -54 \\ c &= -6 \end{aligned}$$

So, the velocity function is $v = t^2 - t - 6$.

gdc.instructions: In Run-Matrix, select OPTN > CALC > ∫dx. Enter X^2-X, 6, 15.

ti84.instructions: Press MATH > 9:fnInt. Enter (X^2 - X), X, 6, 15.

desmos.instructions: Type int(6, 15, t^2 - t) dt

calculator.description: Evaluate the definite integral of (t^2 - t) from 6 to 15

steps.title: Integrate velocity to find displacement
steps.content:
Now we integrate the complete velocity function to find the general expression for displacement $s$.

$$\begin{aligned} s &= \int v \, dt \\ &= \int (t^2 - t - 6) \, dt \\ &= \frac{t^3}{3} - \frac{t^2}{2} - 6t + d \end{aligned}$$

We have a new constant of integration, $d$.

steps.title: Solve for the displacement constant d
steps.content:
Use one of the given boundary conditions to find $d$. We know $s = 18.25$ when $t = 6$.

$$18.25 = \frac{6^3}{3} - \frac{6^2}{2} - 6(6) + d$$

Calculate the values:

$$\begin{aligned} 18.25 &= 72 - 18 - 36 + d \\ 18.25 &= 18 + d \\ d &= 0.25 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the value of the expression at t=6

highlights.text: When $t = 6$, its displacement $s$ from a fixed point P is 18.25 m

steps.title: State the final expression
steps.content:
Substitute $d = 0.25$ (or $\frac{1}{4}$) back into the displacement equation.

$$s = \frac{t^3}{3} - \frac{t^2}{2} - 6t + 0.25$$

Or using fractions:
$$s = \frac{t^3}{3} - \frac{t^2}{2} - 6t + \frac{1}{4}$$

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses an implicitly defined curve (ellipse) with x(t), y(t) and given dx/dt at a point to find possible dy/dt via differentiating with respect to time (related rates under a geometric constraint).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Number and Algebra > SL 1.1—Using standard form > Question Type 1: Converting positive numbers to standard form
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Converting positive numbers to standard form
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Final answers are requested in standard form (scientific notation), so standard-form handling may be assessed as part of the response.

parts.content:
A lunar satellite moves in a path that can be described by the curve $72.5x^2 + 71.5y^2 = 1$ where $x = x(t)$ and $y = y(t)$ are in thousands of kilometres and $t$ is time in seconds.

Given that $\frac{dx}{dt} = 7.75 \times 10^{-5}$ when $x = 3.2 \times 10^{-3}$, find the possible values of $\frac{dy}{dt}$.

Give your answers in standard form.
parts.markscheme:
### Method #1

- Substituting for $x$ and attempting to solve for $y$ <marks>M1</marks>

- $y = \pm 0.11821 \dots$ <marks>A1</marks>

- **EITHER**
  $145x + 143y \frac{dy}{dx} = 0$ (or $\frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{145x}{143y}$) <marks>M1A1</marks>

- **OR**
  $145x \frac{dx}{dt} + 143y \frac{dy}{dt} = 0$ <marks>M1A1</marks>

- Substituting numerical values to find $\frac{dy}{dt}$:
  $\frac{dy}{dt} = -\frac{145(3.2 \times 10^{-3})}{143(\pm 0.11821 \dots)} \times (7.75 \times 10^{-5})$ <marks>M1</marks>

- $\frac{dy}{dt} = \pm 2.13 \times 10^{-6}$ <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Award all marks except the final A1 to candidates who do not consider $\pm$</note>

### Method #2

- $y = \pm \sqrt{\frac{1 - 72.5x^2}{71.5}}$ <marks>M1A1</marks>

- $\frac{dy}{dx} = \pm 0.0274 \dots$ <marks>M1A1</marks>

- $\frac{dy}{dt} = \pm 0.0274 \dots \times 7.75 \times 10^{-5}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- $\frac{dy}{dt} = \pm 2.13 \times 10^{-6}$ <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Award all marks except the final A1 to candidates who do not consider $\pm$</note>

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content: We need to find the possible values of $\frac{dy}{dt}$ using the given equation and rate of change. We will use the **Related Rates** method by differentiating the equation with respect to time $t$. First, let's identify the given information.

steps.title: Solve for y
steps.content:
Substitute the given value of $x$ into the curve equation to find the corresponding values of $y$.

$$\begin{aligned} 72.5(3.2 \times 10^{-3})^2 + 71.5y^2 &= 1 \\ 72.5(1.024 \times 10^{-5}) + 71.5y^2 &= 1 \\ 7.424 \times 10^{-4} + 71.5y^2 &= 1 \\ 71.5y^2 &= 1 - 7.424 \times 10^{-4} \\ y^2 &\approx 0.013976 \\ y &= \pm 0.11821\dots \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the square root expression

ti84.instructions: Enter the equation to solve for y

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to evaluate y

calculator.description: Calculate the value of y

steps.title: Differentiate with respect to time
steps.content:
Differentiate the entire equation $72.5x^2 + 71.5y^2 = 1$ with respect to time $t$. Remember to use the chain rule for both $x(t)$ and $y(t)$.

Using the power rule $\frac{d}{dt}(u^n) = nu^{n-1} \frac{du}{dt}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d}{dt}(72.5x^2) + \frac{d}{dt}(71.5y^2) &= \frac{d}{dt}(1) \\ 72.5(2x) \frac{dx}{dt} + 71.5(2y) \frac{dy}{dt} &= 0 \\ 145x \frac{dx}{dt} + 143y \frac{dy}{dt} &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Rearrange for dy/dt
steps.content:
Isolate $\frac{dy}{dt}$ in the differentiated equation:

$$\begin{aligned} 143y \frac{dy}{dt} &= -145x \frac{dx}{dt} \\ \frac{dy}{dt} &= -\frac{145x}{143y} \frac{dx}{dt} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute values
steps.content:
Substitute the known values ($x$, $y$, and $\frac{dx}{dt}$) into the expression. Since $y$ has two possible values (positive and negative), we will get two answers.

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{dy}{dt} &= -\frac{145(3.2 \times 10^{-3})}{143(\pm 0.11821\dots)} \times (7.75 \times 10^{-5}) \\ \\ &= \mp \frac{0.464}{16.905\dots} \times (7.75 \times 10^{-5}) \\ \\ &= \mp 2.127\dots \times 10^{-6} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Compute the expression

ti84.instructions: Compute the expression

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the fraction

calculator.description: Calculate the final value

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to 3 significant figures and including both possibilities:

$$\frac{dy}{dt} = \pm 2.13 \times 10^{-6}$$

steps.title: Strategy
steps.content:
To find $\frac{dy}{dt}$, we will use the **Chain Rule with Implicit Gradient** method. This involves three main steps:
1.  Find the $y$-coordinate(s) corresponding to the given $x$-value.
2.  Find the gradient $\frac{dy}{dx}$ by differentiating the curve equation implicitly.
3.  Calculate $\frac{dy}{dt}$ using the chain rule formula:

$$ \frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{dy}{dx} \times \frac{dx}{dt} $$

steps.title: Calculate the y-coordinate
steps.content:
First, we substitute the given value $x = 3.2 \times 10^{-3}$ into the equation of the curve to solve for $y$.

$$ 72.5(3.2 \times 10^{-3})^2 + 71.5y^2 = 1 $$

$$ 72.5(1.024 \times 10^{-5}) + 71.5y^2 = 1 $$

$$ 7.424 \times 10^{-4} + 71.5y^2 = 1 $$

Rearranging for $y^2$:

$$ 71.5y^2 = 1 - 0.0007424 $$

$$ y^2 = \frac{0.9992576}{71.5} $$

$$ y \approx \pm 0.118219... $$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the square root of the rearranged equation

ti84.instructions: Calculate the square root of the rearranged equation

desmos.instructions: Calculate the square root of the rearranged equation

calculator.description: Calculate y

steps.title: Find the gradient dy/dx
steps.content:
Next, we differentiate the original equation $72.5x^2 + 71.5y^2 = 1$ with respect to $x$ using implicit differentiation.

Applying the power rule $f(x)=x^n \Rightarrow f'(x)=nx^{n-1}$ to each term:

$$ \frac{d}{dx}(72.5x^2) + \frac{d}{dx}(71.5y^2) = \frac{d}{dx}(1) $$

$$ 2(72.5)x + 2(71.5)y \frac{dy}{dx} = 0 $$

$$ 145x + 143y \frac{dy}{dx} = 0 $$

Rearranging to isolate $\frac{dy}{dx}$:

$$ \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{145x}{143y} $$

steps.title: Apply the Chain Rule
steps.content:
Now we use the chain rule relationship to find $\frac{dy}{dt}$.

$$ \frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{dy}{dx} \times \frac{dx}{dt} $$

Substituting our expression for $\frac{dy}{dx}$:

$$ \frac{dy}{dt} = \left( -\frac{145x}{143y} \right) \times \frac{dx}{dt} $$

steps.title: Calculate the final values
steps.content:
We substitute the known values into the equation:
- $x = 3.2 \times 10^{-3}$
- $y \approx \pm 0.118219$
- $\frac{dx}{dt} = 7.75 \times 10^{-5}$

$$ \frac{dy}{dt} = -\frac{145(3.2 \times 10^{-3})}{143(\pm 0.118219)} \times (7.75 \times 10^{-5}) $$

When $y$ is positive:
$$ \frac{dy}{dt} \approx -2.13 \times 10^{-6} $$

When $y$ is negative (the negative signs cancel):
$$ \frac{dy}{dt} \approx +2.13 \times 10^{-6} $$

The question asks for the answer in standard form.

Possible values: **$\pm 2.13 \times 10^{-6}$**

gdc.instructions: Use the previous result for y

ti84.instructions: Use the stored answer (Ans) for y to compute the result

desmos.instructions: Compute the full expression

calculator.description: Evaluate dy/dt

steps.title: Identify the strategy
steps.content:
To find $\frac{dy}{dt}$, we will use the **Explicit Differentiation** method combined with the Chain Rule.

The Chain Rule relates the rates of change:

$$\frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{dy}{dx} \times \frac{dx}{dt}$$

We are given $\frac{dx}{dt}$ and $x$, so our goal is to:
1. Rearrange the equation to express $y$ as a function of $x$ ($y = f(x)$).
2. Differentiate explicitly to find $\frac{dy}{dx}$.
3. Substitute the given values to find $\frac{dy}{dt}$.

steps.title: Rearrange for y
steps.content:
First, we rearrange the given equation to make $y$ the subject.

$$\begin{aligned} 72.5x^2 + 71.5y^2 &= 1 \\ 71.5y^2 &= 1 - 72.5x^2 \\ y^2 &= \frac{1 - 72.5x^2}{71.5} \\ y &= \pm \sqrt{\frac{1 - 72.5x^2}{71.5}} \end{aligned}$$

Note that there are two possible values for $y$ (positive and negative), which will lead to two possible values for the rate of change.

steps.title: Differentiate explicitly
steps.content:
Now we differentiate $y$ with respect to $x$ using the Chain Rule (specifically the power rule).

Let $y = \pm \left( \frac{1 - 72.5x^2}{71.5} \right)^{\frac{1}{2}}$.

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \pm \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{1 - 72.5x^2}{71.5} \right)^{-\frac{1}{2}} \times \frac{d}{dx}\left( \frac{1 - 72.5x^2}{71.5} \right)$$

steps.title: Simplify the derivative
steps.content:
Calculate the derivative of the inside term:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left( \frac{1 - 72.5x^2}{71.5} \right) = \frac{-145x}{71.5}$$

Substituting this back into our expression for $\frac{dy}{dx}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{dy}{dx} &= \pm \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{1 - 72.5x^2}{71.5} \right)^{-\frac{1}{2}} \times \left( \frac{-145x}{71.5} \right) \\ &= \mp \frac{145x}{143 \sqrt{\frac{1 - 72.5x^2}{71.5}}} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate dy/dx
steps.content:
Substitute $x = 3.2 \times 10^{-3}$ into the derivative formula.

First, calculate the term under the square root:
$$\sqrt{\frac{1 - 72.5(3.2 \times 10^{-3})^2}{71.5}} \approx 0.118219$$

Now find $\frac{dy}{dx}$:
$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \mp \frac{145(3.2 \times 10^{-3})}{143(0.118219)} \approx \mp 0.027447$$

gdc.instructions: Store the intermediate y-value and calculate the derivative.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the value under the square root first, then the full derivative expression.

desmos.instructions: Define x and calculate the expression.

calculator.description: Calculate the value of the derivative dy/dx

steps.title: Solve for dy/dt
steps.content:
Finally, apply the chain rule $\frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{dy}{dx} \times \frac{dx}{dt}$ using the given value $\frac{dx}{dt} = 7.75 \times 10^{-5}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{dy}{dt} &= (\mp 0.027447) \times (7.75 \times 10^{-5}) \\ &= \mp 2.127... \times 10^{-6} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures and writing in standard form:

$$\frac{dy}{dt} = \pm 2.13 \times 10^{-6}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the previous result by dx/dt.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the previous result by dx/dt.

desmos.instructions: Multiply the derivative result by dx/dt.

calculator.description: Calculate final dy/dt

questions.specification: A particle moves along a straight line, where time $t$ is measured in seconds. Units are omitted for $a(t)$, $v(t)$ and $s(t)$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > SL 5.9—Kinematics problems > Question Type 3: Going from acceleration and velocity, to velocity and displacement
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Going from acceleration and velocity, to velocity and displacement
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: This is a kinematics calculus question requiring integrating acceleration to obtain velocity using an initial condition, then integrating velocity to obtain displacement using an initial condition.

parts.content: Given the acceleration of the particle is $a(t) = 3t^2 - 2t + 1$, find the velocity function $v(t)$ if the initial velocity $v(0) = 4$.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to integrate acceleration function: $v(t) = \int (3t^2 - 2t + 1) \, dt$

- Obtain correct integrated function: $v(t) = t^3 - t^2 + t + C$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute initial condition $v(0) = 4$ to find $C$:
  $4 = 0^3 - 0^2 + 0 + C \Rightarrow C = 4$

- State final velocity function: $v(t) = t^3 - t^2 + t + 4$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<examTip>Remember to include the constant of integration C when integrating</examTip>

parts.content: Using the velocity function $v(t) = t^3 - t^2 + t + 4$, find the displacement function $s(t)$ if the initial displacement $s(0) = 0$.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to integrate velocity function: $s(t) = \int (t^3 - t^2 + t + 4) \, dt$

- Obtain correct integrated function: $s(t) = \frac{t^4}{4} - \frac{t^3}{3} + \frac{t^2}{2} + 4t + C$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute initial condition $s(0) = 0$ to find $C$:
  $0 = 0 - 0 + 0 + 0 + C \Rightarrow C = 0$

- State final displacement function: $s(t) = \frac{t^4}{4} - \frac{t^3}{3} + \frac{t^2}{2} + 4t$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award full marks for correct final answer with clear working shown</note>

questions.specification:
In this question, all lengths are in $metres$ and time is in $seconds.$

A particle moves in a straight line with velocity given by $v(t)=2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)+3 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)$, for $0 \leq t \leq 6$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > SL 5.9—Kinematics problems > Question Type 3: Going from acceleration and velocity, to velocity and displacement
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Going from acceleration and velocity, to velocity and displacement
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Main context is 1D kinematics with a given velocity function: (a) acceleration from differentiating v(t), (b) particle at rest by solving v(t)=0, (c) total distance over an interval using integral of speed (requires sign analysis).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > SL 5.9—Kinematics problems > Question Type 4: Difference between distance and displacement
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 4: Difference between distance and displacement
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) explicitly asks for total distance travelled (integral of |v(t)|), distinguishing distance vs displacement.

parts.content: Find the acceleration of the particle at $t=1.5$.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognizing acceleration is the derivative of velocity
  
	e.g. $a=\frac{d}{d t}\left(2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)+3 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)\right)$ <marks>A1</marks>
- $a(t)=2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{3} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)-3 \cdot \frac{\pi}{3} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)=\frac{2 \pi}{3} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)-\pi \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)$ <marks>A1</marks>
- At $t=1.5, a(1.5)=\frac{2 \pi}{3} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3} \cdot 1.5\right)-\pi \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3} \cdot 1.5\right)=\frac{2 \pi}{3} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)-\pi \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=$
 
  $0-\pi \approx-3.14 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Determine the time $t$ when the particle is at rest in the interval $0 \leq t \leq 6$.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognizing particle is at rest when $v=0$ 
    
	e.g. $2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)+3 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)=0$ 
 
   $\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)=-\frac{3}{2}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- First reference angle: $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) \approx 0.9828$ radians.
   
-  Since $\tan\left(\frac{\pi}{3}t\right)$ is negative, $\frac{\pi}{3}t$ is in Quadrant II or IV.
      
	$\frac{\pi}{3} t = \pi - \alpha + k\pi$ or $\frac{\pi}{3} t = 2\pi - \alpha + k\pi$. (General solution form)
    
	$\frac{\pi}{3} t \approx \pi - 0.9828 \approx 2.1588$ or $\frac{\pi}{3} t \approx 2\pi - 0.9828 \approx 5.2996$
        $t \approx \frac{3}{\pi} (2.1588) \approx 2.06$ seconds <marks>A1</marks>
- $t \approx \frac{3}{\pi} (5.2996) \approx 5.06$ seconds <marks>A1</marks>

Note: Award <marks>A1</marks> for each correct time. Award <marks>A0</marks> if additional solutions outside $0 \leq t \leq 6$ are included.

parts.content: Calculate the total distance travelled by the particle from $t=0$ to $t=3$.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognizing total distance requires absolute value of velocity 
-  Velocity changes sign at $t \approx 2.06$ (from part 2). 
- Distance $= \int_{0}^{2.06}\left(2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)+3 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)\right) d t - \int_{2.06}^{3}\left(2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)+3 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)\right) d t$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Find the correct antiderivative of $v(t)$:
        $\int\left(2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)+3 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)\right) d t = 2 \cdot \frac{-3}{\pi} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right) + 3 \cdot \frac{3}{\pi} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)$
        $= -\frac{6}{\pi} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right) + \frac{9}{\pi} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Evaluate the definite integrals using the antiderivative:
        $\left[-\frac{6}{\pi} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right) + \frac{9}{\pi} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)\right]_{0}^{2.06} - \left[-\frac{6}{\pi} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right) + \frac{9}{\pi} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3} t\right)\right]_{2.06}^{3}$
        $\approx 4.81 - (-0.955)$ (Values from numerical evaluation of integrals)
        $\approx 5.77 \mathrm{~m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

questions.specification: A conical tank with height 12 metres and base radius 4 metres is being filled with water at a rate of 2 cubic metres per minute. The water forms a smaller cone within the tank. The height of the water is increasing as the tank is being filled.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > AHL 5.14—Implicit functions, related rates, optimisation > Question Type 3: Related rates with geometric constraints
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Related rates with geometric constraints
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: This is a classic related-rates problem: water volume in a conical tank increases at a given dV/dt, and the question asks for dh/dt using similar triangles (geometric constraint) between radius and height.

parts.content: Find the rate at which the height of the water is increasing when the height of the water is 6 metres.
parts.markscheme:
- **Express** radius in terms of height: $\frac{r}{h} = \frac{4}{12} \Rightarrow r = \frac{1}{3} h$ <marks>A1</marks>

- **Substitute** into volume formula ($V = \frac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h$): $V = \frac{1}{3} \pi \left(\frac{h}{3}\right)^2 h = \frac{\pi}{27} h^3$ <marks>A1</marks>

- **Differentiate** with respect to $t$: $\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{\pi}{9} h^2 \frac{dh}{dt}$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

- **Substitute** given values ($h=6, \frac{dV}{dt}=2$): $2 = \frac{\pi}{9} (6)^2 \frac{dh}{dt}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- **Solve** for $\frac{dh}{dt}$: $\frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{1}{2\pi}$ metres per minute <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content:
First, let's list the information given in the question and what we need to find.

**Given:**
*   Tank dimensions: Height $H = 12$ m, Radius $R = 4$ m
*   Rate of change of volume: $\frac{dV}{dt} = 2$ m$^3$/min
*   Current water height: $h = 6$ m

**Goal:**
*   Find the rate of change of height: $\frac{dh}{dt}$

highlights.text: rate of 2 cubic metres per minute
height of the water is 6 metres

steps.title: Express radius in terms of height
steps.content:
Since the water forms a cone similar to the tank, the ratio of the radius to the height is constant. We use similar triangles to express the water's radius $r$ in terms of its height $h$.

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{r}{h} &= \frac{\text{tank radius}}{\text{tank height}} \\ \frac{r}{h} &= \frac{4}{12} \\ r &= \frac{1}{3}h \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute into the volume formula
steps.content:
The volume of a cone is given by $V = \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h$. 

We substitute our expression $r = \frac{1}{3}h$ into this formula to create an equation for Volume solely in terms of height ($h$).

$$\begin{aligned} V &= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left(\frac{h}{3}\right)^2 h \\ V &= \frac{1}{3}\pi \left(\frac{h^2}{9}\right) h \\ V &= \frac{\pi}{27}h^3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Differentiate with respect to time
steps.content:
Now we differentiate both sides with respect to time $t$. Since $h$ is a function of time, we must use the chain rule (implicit differentiation) for the right-hand side.

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d}{dt}(V) &= \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\pi}{27}h^3\right) \\ \frac{dV}{dt} &= \frac{\pi}{27} \cdot 3h^2 \cdot \frac{dh}{dt} \\ \frac{dV}{dt} &= \frac{\pi}{9}h^2 \frac{dh}{dt} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute values and solve
steps.content:
We substitute the known values $\frac{dV}{dt} = 2$ and $h = 6$ into our differentiated equation to solve for $\frac{dh}{dt}$.

$$\begin{aligned} 2 &= \frac{\pi}{9}(6)^2 \frac{dh}{dt} \\ 2 &= \frac{\pi}{9}(36) \frac{dh}{dt} \\ 2 &= 4\pi \frac{dh}{dt} \\ \frac{dh}{dt} &= \frac{2}{4\pi} \\ \frac{dh}{dt} &= \frac{1}{2\pi} \end{aligned}$$

The rate at which the height is increasing is $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ m/min.

gdc.instructions: Calculate 2 divided by (4*pi)

ti84.instructions: Calculate 2 divided by (4*pi) to verify the decimal value if needed

desmos.instructions: Type 2/(4pi)

calculator.description: Verify the algebraic simplification

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
First, let's list the values and rates given in the question. We are dealing with a cone and looking for the rate of change of height.

*   Rate of change of volume: $\frac{dV}{dt} = 2$ m$^3$/min
*   Tank dimensions: Height $12$ m, Radius $4$ m
*   Current water height: $h = 6$ m
*   Target: Find $\frac{dh}{dt}$

highlights.text: height 12 metres and base radius 4 metres
rate of 2 cubic metres per minute
height of the water is 6 metres

steps.title: State the volume formula
steps.content:
The volume of a cone is given by the formula:

$$V = \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h$$

Since we are using the **Differentiate then Substitute** method, we will differentiate this formula directly with respect to time $t$, treating both $r$ and $h$ as functions of time.

steps.title: Differentiate volume with respect to time
steps.content:
Using the product rule on the terms $r^2$ and $h$, we differentiate implicitly with respect to time:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{\pi}{3} \left( \underbrace{2r \frac{dr}{dt} \cdot h}_{\text{derivative of } r^2 \times h} + \underbrace{r^2 \cdot \frac{dh}{dt}}_{r^2 \times \text{derivative of } h} \right)$$

steps.title: Relate radius and height
steps.content:
We need to find $r$ and $\frac{dr}{dt}$ to substitute into our equation. We use similar triangles formed by the water cone and the tank cone.

$$\frac{r}{h} = \frac{\text{radius of tank}}{\text{height of tank}} = \frac{4}{12}$$

Simplifying this gives:

$$r = \frac{1}{3}h$$

highlights.text: height 12 metres and base radius 4 metres

steps.title: Relate the rates of change
steps.content:
Now we differentiate the relationship $r = \frac{1}{3}h$ with respect to time to find the relationship between the rates:

$$\frac{dr}{dt} = \frac{1}{3} \frac{dh}{dt}$$

steps.title: Determine values at h = 6
steps.content:
At the specific instant when $h = 6$, we calculate the corresponding radius:

$$r = \frac{1}{3}(6) = 2$$

Now we have all the components to substitute into our differentiated volume equation:
*   $\frac{dV}{dt} = 2$
*   $h = 6$
*   $r = 2$
*   $\frac{dr}{dt} = \frac{1}{3}\frac{dh}{dt}$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 6 divided by 3

ti84.instructions: Calculate 6 divided by 3

desmos.instructions: Calculate 6 divided by 3

calculator.description: Calculate radius at height 6

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Substitute these values into the equation from Step 2:

$$2 = \frac{\pi}{3} \left( 2(2)\left(\frac{1}{3}\frac{dh}{dt}\right)(6) + (2)^2\frac{dh}{dt} \right)$$

Simplify the terms inside the brackets:

$$\begin{aligned} 2 &= \frac{\pi}{3} \left( 4(2)\frac{dh}{dt} + 4\frac{dh}{dt} \right) \\ 2 &= \frac{\pi}{3} \left( 8\frac{dh}{dt} + 4\frac{dh}{dt} \right) \\ 2 &= \frac{\pi}{3} \left( 12\frac{dh}{dt} \right) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Final calculation
steps.content:
Finally, solve for $\frac{dh}{dt}$:

$$2 = 4\pi \frac{dh}{dt}$$

$$\frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{2}{4\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \text{ m min}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Check that (pi/3) * 12 equals 4*pi

ti84.instructions: Check that (pi/3) * 12 equals 4*pi

desmos.instructions: Check that (pi/3) * 12 equals 4*pi

calculator.description: Verify the algebraic simplification

steps.title: Strategy: Solving via Radius Rate
steps.content:
This is a related rates problem. We are given the rate of change of volume $\frac{dV}{dt}$ and need to find the rate of change of height $\frac{dh}{dt}$.

Using the **Solving via Radius Rate** approach, we will:
1. Find the relationship between radius $r$ and height $h$.
2. Express the volume $V$ in terms of the radius $r$ only.
3. Differentiate to find $\frac{dr}{dt}$.
4. Convert $\frac{dr}{dt}$ to $\frac{dh}{dt}$ using the geometric relationship.

steps.title: Establish the geometric relationship
steps.content:
The water forms a cone similar to the tank itself. We use similar triangles to relate the radius $r$ and height $h$. 

The tank has a total height of 12 m and a radius of 4 m.

$$ \frac{r}{h} = \frac{\text{radius of tank}}{\text{height of tank}} = \frac{4}{12} $$

Simplifying this ratio:
$$ \frac{r}{h} = \frac{1}{3} \implies h = 3r $$

highlights.text: height 12 metres and base radius 4 metres

steps.title: Express Volume in terms of radius
steps.content:
The formula for the volume of a cone is:

$$ V = \frac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h $$

Since we want to find the rate for the radius first, we substitute $h = 3r$ into the volume formula to express $V$ entirely in terms of $r$:

$$ V = \frac{1}{3} \pi r^2 (3r) $$
$$ V = \pi r^3 $$

steps.title: Differentiate with respect to time
steps.content:
Now we differentiate the volume equation $V = \pi r^3$ with respect to time $t$ using the chain rule.

$$ \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}(\pi r^3) $$

$$ \frac{dV}{dt} = 3\pi r^2 \frac{dr}{dt} $$

steps.title: Calculate the radius at the specific instant
steps.content:
We need to evaluate this rate when the water height is 6 metres. First, we must find the radius $r$ at this instant using our relationship $r = \frac{h}{3}$.

When $h = 6$:
$$ r = \frac{6}{3} = 2 \text{ metres} $$

highlights.text: height of the water is 6 metres

steps.title: Solve for the rate of change of radius
steps.content:
We are given that water fills the tank at 2 cubic metres per minute, so $\frac{dV}{dt} = 2$. Substitute $r=2$ and $\frac{dV}{dt}=2$ into our differentiated equation:

$$ 2 = 3\pi (2)^2 \frac{dr}{dt} $$

$$ 2 = 12\pi \frac{dr}{dt} $$

Solving for $\frac{dr}{dt}$:
$$ \frac{dr}{dt} = \frac{2}{12\pi} = \frac{1}{6\pi} \text{ m/min} $$

gdc.instructions: Enter 1/(6*pi)

ti84.instructions: Enter 1 divided by (6*pi)

desmos.instructions: Type 1/(6pi)

calculator.description: Calculate the numerical value of dr/dt to verify logic (Optional)

highlights.text: rate of 2 cubic metres per minute

steps.title: Convert radius rate to height rate
steps.content:
Finally, we convert $\frac{dr}{dt}$ to the required $\frac{dh}{dt}$.

Recall the relationship $h = 3r$. Differentiating this with respect to $t$ gives:

$$ \frac{dh}{dt} = 3 \frac{dr}{dt} $$

Substitute our value for $\frac{dr}{dt}$:

$$ \frac{dh}{dt} = 3 \left( \frac{1}{6\pi} \right) $$

$$ \frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{3}{6\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \text{ metres per minute} $$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 3 * (1/(6*pi))

ti84.instructions: Calculate 3 * (1/(6*pi))

desmos.instructions: Type 3 * (1/(6pi))

calculator.description: Verify the final numerical answer

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The rate at which the height of the water is increasing is:

$$ \frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \text{ m/min} $$

questions.specification: Two particles, A and B, move in a straight line. Particle A has displacement $s_{A}(t)=$ $4 t-\frac{1}{2} t^{2}$, and particle B moves with constant velocity $v_{B}=2 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, for $t \geq 0$. All lengths are in metres and time is in seconds.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > SL 5.9—Kinematics problems > Question Type 1: Given the displacement or velocity, finding the velocity or acceleration respectively

questions.specification:
In this question, all lengths are in metres and time is in seconds.
A particle moves along a straight line with velocity given by

$$
v(t)= \begin{cases}3 t-\frac{1}{2} t^{2}, & 0 \leq t 
\leq 2 \\ \frac{4}{t-1} - 2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right), & t>2\end{cases}
$$

The particle is at the origin at $t=0$.

parts.content: Find the displacement of the particle at $t=2$.
parts.markscheme:
- Integrating velocity for $0 \leq t \leq 2$
<marks>M1</marks>

- $s(t)=\int_{0}^{2}\left(3 t-\frac{1}{2} t^{2}\right) d t=\left[\frac{3}{2} t^{2}-\frac{1}{6} t^{3}\right]_{0}^{2}$
<marks>A1</marks>

- Substitution: $\left(\frac{3}{2} \cdot 4-\frac{1}{6} \cdot 8\right)-0=6-\frac{4}{3}=\frac{14}{3} \approx 4.67 \mathrm{~m}$
<marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the relevant formula
steps.content:
To find the displacement from velocity, we need to integrate the velocity function. Since the particle starts at the origin ($s(0)=0$), the displacement at time $t=2$ is the definite integral of velocity from $t=0$ to $t=2$.

We look at the velocity function defined in the question and select the part valid for the interval $0 \leq t \leq 2$:

$$v(t) = 3t - \frac{1}{2}t^2$$

steps.title: Set up the definite integral
steps.content:
We set up the integral for the displacement $s(2)$:

$$s(2) = \int_{0}^{2} \left( 3t - \frac{1}{2}t^2 \right) \, dt$$

gdc.instructions: In Run-Matrix mode, press [OPTN] > [CALC] > [∫dx]. Enter ∫(3x - 0.5x^2, 0, 2).

ti84.instructions: Press [MATH] > 9:fnInt(. Enter fnInt(3X - 0.5X^2, X, 0, 2).

desmos.instructions: Type int(0, 2, 3t - 0.5t^2) dt

calculator.description: Calculate the definite integral to verify the result.

steps.title: Apply power rule for integration
steps.content:
We integrate each term using the power rule formula $\int x^n dx = \frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \int 3t \, dt &= 3 \cdot \frac{t^2}{2} = \frac{3}{2}t^2 \\ \int -\frac{1}{2}t^2 \, dt &= -\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{t^3}{3} = -\frac{1}{6}t^3 \end{aligned}$$

Combining these gives the integrated function:

$$\left[ \frac{3}{2}t^2 - \frac{1}{6}t^3 \right]_{0}^{2}$$

steps.title: Substitute limits to solve
steps.content:
Now we evaluate the integral by substituting the upper limit ($t=2$) and lower limit ($t=0$):

$$\begin{aligned} s(2) &= \left( \frac{3}{2}(2)^2 - \frac{1}{6}(2)^3 \right) - \left( \frac{3}{2}(0)^2 - \frac{1}{6}(0)^3 \right) \\ &= \left( \frac{3}{2}(4) - \frac{1}{6}(8) \right) - 0 \\ &= 6 - \frac{8}{6} \\ &= 6 - \frac{4}{3} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 6 - 4/3

ti84.instructions: Enter 6 - 4/3

desmos.instructions: Enter 6 - 4/3

calculator.description: Calculate the final numerical value.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the exact value:

$$\begin{aligned} s(2) &= \frac{18}{3} - \frac{4}{3} \\ &= \frac{14}{3} \end{aligned}$$

The displacement is $\frac{14}{3}$ m (or approximately $4.67$ m).

steps.title: Identify the correct function
steps.content:
To find the displacement at $t=2$, we need to integrate the velocity function from $t=0$ to $t=2$. 

Looking at the piecewise function defined in the question, the time interval $0 \leq t \leq 2$ corresponds to the first expression:

$$v(t) = 3t - \frac{1}{2}t^2$$

steps.title: Set up the integral
steps.content:
Displacement is the definite integral of velocity over time. Since the particle starts at the origin at $t=0$, the displacement at $t=2$ is simply the integral from $0$ to $2$:

$$s(2) = \int_{0}^{2} \left(3t - \frac{1}{2}t^2\right) \, dt$$

The calculator gives a result of approximately $4.666...$. 

Converting this to a fraction gives $\frac{14}{3}$, or you can write the decimal to 3 significant figures.

$$s(2) \approx 4.67 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Identify the condition for rest
steps.content:
To find when the particle comes to rest, we need to find the time $t$ when the velocity is zero ($v(t) = 0$).

The question asks for a time $t > 2$, so we must use the second part of the piecewise function:

$$v(t) = \frac{4}{t-1} - 2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right)$$

steps.title: Graph the velocity function
steps.content:
Since this is a complex equation involving both algebraic and trigonometric terms, the most efficient method is to graph the function on your calculator and find the $x$-intercept.

Make sure your calculator is in **Radian** mode because the sine function contains $\pi$.

steps.title: Identify the condition for rest
steps.content:
To determine when the particle comes to rest, we must find the time $t$ when its velocity is zero. The question asks for the first time $t > 2$.

$$v(t) = 0$$

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
Substitute the expression for velocity given for $t > 2$ into the equation. 

$$\frac{4}{t-1} - 2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right) = 0$$

To use the **Intersection of Functions** approach, we rearrange this equation by adding the sine term to both sides, creating two separate functions equal to each other:

$$\frac{4}{t-1} = 2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right)$$

steps.title: Graph the functions
steps.content:
We define two functions, $y_1$ and $y_2$, representing each side of the equation. We will graph these on a calculator and look for their intersection point.

$$\begin{aligned} y_1 &= \frac{4}{t-1} \\ y_2 &= 2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right) \end{aligned}$$

6. Select G-SOLVE (or ISCT) to find the intersection

7. Move cursor to the intersection point and press [ENTER] three times

3. Click on the point where the two curves cross for x > 2

Using the calculator's intersection tool, we find the coordinates of the point where the two graphs cross. We are looking for the $t$-value (or $x$-value) of this point.

The intersection occurs at:

Rounding to 3 significant figures, we get:
$$t \approx 3.32$$

parts.content: Calculate the total distance travelled by the particle from $t=0$ to $t=4$.
parts.markscheme:
- Distance $=\int_{0}^{2}\left|3 t-\frac{1}{2} t^{2}\right| d t+\int_{2}^{4}\left|\frac{4}{t-1} - 2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right)\right| d t$
<marks>M1</marks>

- For $0 \leq t \leq 2, v(t) \geq 0$, so $\int_{0}^{2}\left(3 t-\frac{1}{2} t^{2}\right) d t=\frac{14}{3}$ (from part (a))
<marks>A1</marks>

- For $t>2$, velocity zero at $t \approx 3.32$. Distance $=\int_{2}^{3.32} v(t) d t - \int_{3.32}^{4} v(t) d t$
<marks>A1</marks>

- Antiderivative: $\int\left(\frac{4}{t-1} - 2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right)\right) d t= 4 \ln |t-1| + \frac{8}{\pi} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right)$. Evaluate integrals: $\approx 2.12 + 0.27 \approx 2.39$
<marks>M1</marks>

- Total distance $\approx \frac{14}{3} + 2.39 \approx 7.05 \mathrm{~m}$
<marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

steps.title: Recall the distance formula
steps.content:
To find the total distance travelled, we must integrate the speed (the magnitude of velocity) over the given time interval. Unlike displacement, which integrates velocity directly, distance requires the absolute value of velocity.

$$ \text{Distance} = \int_{0}^{4} |v(t)| \, dt $$

steps.title: Split the integral
steps.content:
Since the velocity function $v(t)$ is defined piecewise with a boundary at $t=2$, we must split the integral into two parts:

$$ \text{Distance} = \int_{0}^{2} |v(t)| \, dt + \int_{2}^{4} |v(t)| \, dt $$

steps.title: Calculate the first integral
steps.content:
For the interval $0 \leq t \leq 2$, the velocity is $v(t) = 3t - \frac{1}{2}t^2$. Using your GDC, calculate the integral of the absolute value.

$$ \int_{0}^{2} \left|3t - \frac{1}{2}t^2\right| \, dt = \frac{14}{3} \approx 4.6667 $$

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix or Graph menu, select the integration tool.

ti84.instructions: Press MATH > 9 (fnInt). Enter the absolute value function using MATH > NUM > 1 (abs).

desmos.instructions: Type 'int' for the integral symbol and '|' for absolute value.

calculator.description: Calculate the first integral using numerical integration.

steps.title: Calculate the second integral
steps.content:
For the interval $2 < t \leq 4$, the velocity is $v(t) = \frac{4}{t-1} - 2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right)$.

Use your GDC to integrate the absolute value of this function directly. Using the absolute value function is crucial because the velocity becomes negative within this interval (at $t \approx 3.32$), and we need the total distance.

$$ \int_{2}^{4} \left|\frac{4}{t-1} - 2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right)\right| \, dt \approx 2.3841 $$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the integral of the absolute value for the second interval.

ti84.instructions: Enter the second part of the piecewise function inside the absolute value.

desmos.instructions: Type the second integral.

calculator.description: Calculate the second integral. Ensure your calculator is in RADIAN mode.

steps.title: Sum for total distance
steps.content:
Add the two results together to find the total distance. Be sure to keep high precision before rounding.

$$ \text{Distance} \approx 4.6667 + 2.3841 = 7.0508 $$

gdc.instructions: Add the results.

ti84.instructions: Add the two calculated values.

desmos.instructions: Add the results.

calculator.description: Sum the previous results.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to 3 significant figures:

$$ \text{Distance} = 7.05 \text{ m} $$

steps.title: Identify the distance formula
steps.content:
To find the total distance travelled, we must integrate the absolute value of the velocity function over the given time interval $[0, 4]$.

$$\text{Distance} = \int_{0}^{4} |v(t)| \, dt$$

Since the velocity is defined by a piecewise function, we calculate the distance for each part separately:
1. For $0 \leq t \leq 2$, using the first function.
2. For $2 < t \leq 4$, using the second function.

steps.title: Calculate distance for first interval
steps.content:
For $0 \leq t \leq 2$, the velocity is $v(t) = 3t - \frac{1}{2}t^2$. We check if the velocity changes sign in this interval by finding the roots:

$$t(3 - 0.5t) = 0 \implies t=0 \text{ or } t=6$$

Since there are no roots between 0 and 2, the velocity does not change sign. Testing $t=1$, $v(1) = 2.5 > 0$, so we can integrate directly:

$$\begin{aligned} \int_{0}^{2} \left(3t - \frac{1}{2}t^2\right) \, dt &= \left[ \frac{3t^2}{2} - \frac{t^3}{6} \right]_{0}^{2} \\ &= \left(\frac{3(4)}{2} - \frac{8}{6}\right) - 0 \\ &= 6 - \frac{4}{3} \\ &= \frac{14}{3} \approx 4.67 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In Run-Matrix mode, press OPTN > CALC > ∫dx. Enter ∫(3x - 0.5x^2, 0, 2).

ti84.instructions: Press MATH > 9:fnInt. Enter fnInt(3X - 0.5X^2, X, 0, 2).

desmos.instructions: Type 'int' to create an integral, set bounds 0 to 2, and enter (3x - 0.5x^2) dx.

calculator.description: Verify the definite integral calculation

steps.title: Find the root for the second interval
steps.content:
For $t > 2$, the velocity is $v(t) = \frac{4}{t-1} - 2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right)$. We must check if the velocity becomes zero (changes sign) in the interval $[2, 4]$.

Use your graphing calculator to solve for $v(t) = 0$.

$$ \frac{4}{t-1} - 2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right) = 0 $$

gdc.instructions: In Graph mode, enter Y1 = 4/(x-1) - 2sin(π/4 * (x-2)). Draw and use G-Solv > Root.

ti84.instructions: Press Y=. Enter Y1 = 4/(X-1) - 2sin(π/4 * (X-2)). Graph in window X[2, 4]. Press 2nd > TRACE (CALC) > 2:zero.

desmos.instructions: Graph y = 4/(x-1) - 2sin(π/4 * (x-2)). Click the intersection with the x-axis between 2 and 4.

calculator.description: Find the root of the velocity function in the interval [2, 4]

steps.title: Interpret the calculator result
steps.content:
The calculator shows a root at approximately **$t \approx 3.32$**.

This means the particle changes direction at $t \approx 3.32$ seconds. To calculate total distance, we must split the integral at this root:
- From $t=2$ to $t=3.32$, velocity is positive.
- From $t=3.32$ to $t=4$, velocity is negative.

Therefore, we will calculate:

$$ \text{Distance}_2 = \int_{2}^{3.32} v(t) \, dt - \int_{3.32}^{4} v(t) \, dt $$

steps.title: Find the antiderivative for the second part
steps.content:
To evaluate these integrals analytically, we find the indefinite integral (antiderivative) of $v(t)$.

1. For $\frac{4}{t-1}$, the integral is $4 \ln|t-1|$.
2. For $-2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right)$, we use the reverse chain rule. The integral of $\sin(u)$ is $-\cos(u)$, and we divide by the coefficient of $t$, which is $\frac{\pi}{4}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \int -2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right) dt &= -2 \left( -\frac{1}{\pi/4} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right) \right) \\ &= \frac{8}{\pi} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right) \end{aligned}$$

Combining these:
$$F(t) = 4 \ln|t-1| + \frac{8}{\pi} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right)$$

steps.title: Evaluate the definite integrals
steps.content:
Now we substitute the limits into $F(t)$. Let $R \approx 3.32$.

**Values of F(t):**
- $F(2) = 4 \ln(1) + \frac{8}{\pi} \cos(0) = 0 + \frac{8}{\pi} \approx 2.55$
- $F(4) = 4 \ln(3) + \frac{8}{\pi} \cos(\frac{\pi}{2}) = 4 \ln(3) + 0 \approx 4.39$
- $F(3.32) \approx 4.66$ (using calculator)

**Calculate Areas:**
- Area 1 ($2 \to 3.32$): $F(3.32) - F(2) \approx 4.66 - 2.55 = 2.11$
- Area 2 ($3.32 \to 4$): $|F(4) - F(3.32)| = |4.39 - 4.66| = 0.27$

Total distance for part 2:
$$ 2.11 + 0.27 = 2.38 \text{ m} $$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 4*ln(2.32) + (8/π)*cos(π/4 * 1.32)

desmos.instructions: Calculate 4*ln(2.32) + (8/pi)*cos(pi/4 * 1.32)

calculator.description: Calculate the value of the antiderivative at the root

steps.title: Calculate total distance
steps.content:
Finally, add the distances from both time intervals.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Total Distance} &= \text{Distance}_1 + \text{Distance}_2 \\ &= \frac{14}{3} + 2.38 \\ &\approx 4.67 + 2.38 \\ &= 7.05 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Sum the final values

parts.content: Find the acceleration of the particle at $t=3$.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to find $a(t)$ for $t>2$: $a(t)=\frac{d}{d t}\left(\frac{4}{t-1} - 2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right)\right)$
<marks>M1</marks>

- $a(t) = -\frac{4}{(t-1)^{2}} - \frac{\pi}{2} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right)$
<marks>A1</marks>

- At $t=3, a(3) = -\frac{4}{(3-1)^{2}} - \frac{\pi}{2} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = -1 - \frac{\pi\sqrt{2}}{4} \approx -2.11 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}$
<marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Select velocity function
steps.content:
Since we need the acceleration at $t=3$ and $3 > 2$, we select the second part of the piecewise velocity function.

$$v(t) = \frac{4}{t-1} - 2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right)$$

steps.title: Differentiate algebraic term
steps.content:
Acceleration is the derivative of velocity, $a(t) = v'(t)$. We differentiate the first term $\frac{4}{t-1}$ by rewriting it as $4(t-1)^{-1}$ and applying the power rule.

Using the formula from the **Calculus** section:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(4(t-1)^{-1}\right) = -1 \cdot 4(t-1)^{-2} \cdot 1 = -\frac{4}{(t-1)^2}$$

steps.title: Differentiate trigonometric term
steps.content:
Next, we differentiate the second term $-2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right)$. We use the derivative of sine and apply the chain rule for the inner function $\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)$.

Using the formula from the **Calculus** section:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(-2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right)\right) = -2 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right) \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} = -\frac{\pi}{2} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right)$$

steps.title: State acceleration function
steps.content:
Combining the derivatives of both terms gives the complete acceleration function for $t > 2$:

$$a(t) = -\frac{4}{(t-1)^2} - \frac{\pi}{2} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right)$$

steps.title: Substitute time value
steps.content:
Substitute $t=3$ into the acceleration function:

$$\begin{aligned} a(3) &= -\frac{4}{(3-1)^2} - \frac{\pi}{2} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(3-2)\right) \\ &= -\frac{4}{2^2} - \frac{\pi}{2} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Evaluate final answer
steps.content:
Calculate the final value. Recall that $\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$ (or $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$).

$$\begin{aligned} a(3) &= -\frac{4}{4} - \frac{\pi}{2} \left(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right) \\ &= -1 - \frac{\pi\sqrt{2}}{4} \\ &\approx -2.11 \mathrm{~m\,s^{-2}} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. In Run-Matrix mode
2. Enter: -1 - (π × √(2)) ÷ 4
3. Press [EXE]

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [ON]
2. Enter: -1 - (π * √(2)) / 4
3. Press [ENTER]

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly into an input line

steps.title: Select the correct velocity function
steps.content:
Since we need to find the acceleration at $t=3$, we must identify which part of the piecewise velocity function applies. The question defines the velocity for $t > 2$ as:

$$v(t) = \frac{4}{t-1} - 2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(t-2)\right)$$

Because $3 > 2$, we use this expression.

steps.title: Relate acceleration to velocity
steps.content:
Acceleration is defined as the rate of change of velocity with respect to time. In calculus terms, this is the derivative of the velocity function:

$$a(t) = v'(t) = \frac{d}{dt}(v(t))$$

Therefore, we need to calculate the derivative of $v(t)$ at exactly $t=3$.

steps.title: Use GDC numerical derivative
steps.content:
Instead of differentiating analytically, we can use the **numerical derivative** feature on the graphing display calculator (GDC) to evaluate the slope of the velocity curve directly at $t=3$.

Ensure your calculator is set to **Radian** mode before proceeding.

2. Press [OPTN] > [CALC] > [d/dx]
3. Enter the function: 4/(x-1) - 2sin((π/4)(x-2))
4. Set x = 3
5. Press [EXE]

2. Enter the variable: X
3. Enter the function: 4/(X-1) - 2sin((π/4)(X-2))
4. Enter the value to evaluate at: 3
5. Press [ENTER]

desmos.instructions:
1. Define v(t) = 4/(t-1) - 2sin((π/4)(t-2))
2. Type 'v'(3)' or '(d/dt v(t))|_{t=3}' to evaluate

calculator.description: Calculate the numerical derivative at x=3

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator yields a value of approximately $-2.1107...$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, the acceleration is:

$$a(3) \approx -2.11 \text{ m s}^{-2}$$

questions.specification: Two particles, A and B, move in a straight line. Particle A has displacement $s_{A}(t)= 4 t-\frac{1}{2} t^{2}$, and particle B moves with constant velocity $v_{B}=2 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, for $t \geq 0$. All lengths are in metres and time is in seconds.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > SL 5.9—Kinematics problems > Question Type 1: Given the displacement or velocity, finding the velocity or acceleration respectively
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Given the displacement or velocity, finding the velocity or acceleration respectively
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: This is a 1D kinematics question: velocity is found by differentiating displacement, and then solving for time when particle speeds match a given constant speed.

parts.content: Find the velocity of particle A at time $t$.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognizing velocity is the derivative of displacement <marks>M1</marks>
- e.g. $v_{A}=\frac{d}{d t}\left(4 t-\frac{1}{2} t^{2}\right)$
- $v_{A}=4-t$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.content:
To find the velocity of particle A, we need to understand the relationship between displacement and velocity. In kinematics, instantaneous velocity is defined as the rate of change of displacement with respect to time.

Therefore, we need to find the derivative of the displacement function $s_A(t)$ with respect to $t$:

$$v_A(t) = \frac{ds_A}{dt}$$

steps.title: Differentiate using power rule
steps.content:
We differentiate each term of the displacement function using the power rule formula from the Calculus section of the data booklet:

$$f(x)=x^{n} \Rightarrow f'(x)=nx^{n-1}$$

Applying this to our function $s_A(t) = 4t - \frac{1}{2}t^2$:

1.  For the term $4t$ (which is $4t^1$): the derivative is $1 \cdot 4t^{1-1} = 4t^0 = 4$.
2.  For the term $-\frac{1}{2}t^2$: the derivative is $2 \cdot (-\frac{1}{2})t^{2-1} = -1t = -t$.

Combining these gives:

$$v_A(t) = 4 - t$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The velocity of particle A at time $t$ is:

$$v_A = 4 - t$$

This matches the markscheme requirement for finding the derivative of displacement.

steps.title: Identify the displacement function
steps.content: We are given the displacement function for particle A. Since this is a quadratic function of time ($t^2$), the particle moves with constant acceleration.

steps.title: Compare to kinematic formula
steps.content:
To find the velocity, we can compare the given function to the standard kinematic equation for displacement with constant acceleration:

$$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$

Matching the terms with $$s_A(t) = 4t - \frac{1}{2}t^2$$:
- The term with $t$ corresponds to the initial velocity $u$.
- The term with $t^2$ corresponds to $\frac{1}{2}a$.

steps.title: Determine initial velocity and acceleration
steps.content:
By comparing the coefficients:

1.  For the $t$ term: $$u = 4$$

2.  For the $t^2$ term:
    $$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{2}a &= -\frac{1}{2} \\ a &= -1 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate -0.5 / 0.5

ti84.instructions: Calculate -0.5 / 0.5

desmos.instructions: Calculate -0.5 / 0.5

calculator.description: Calculate acceleration

steps.title: Substitute into velocity equation
steps.content:
Now we use the kinematic equation for velocity, $v = u + at$, and substitute our values $u=4$ and $a=-1$:

$$\begin{aligned} v_A(t) &= 4 + (-1)t \\ v_A(t) &= 4 - t \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The velocity of particle A at time $t$ is:

$$v_A = 4 - t$$

This matches the result obtained by differentiating the displacement, where $v = \frac{ds}{dt}$.

parts.content: Determine the time $t$ when the speeds of particles A and B are equal.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognizing speed of A is $|4-t|$ and speed of B is 2 <marks>M1</marks>
- Setting up equation $|4-t|=2$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Solving: $4-t=2$ or $4-t=-2$
- $t=2$ or $t=6$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Award A1 for either correct time, but both must be given for full marks.</note>

steps.title: Find velocity of particle A
steps.content:
To compare speeds, we first need the velocity of particle A. We find velocity by differentiating the displacement function $s_A(t)$ with respect to time.

Using the power rule $\frac{d}{dt}(at^n) = nat^{n-1}$:

$$\begin{aligned} s_A(t) &= 4t - \frac{1}{2}t^2 \\ v_A(t) &= \frac{ds_A}{dt} \\ v_A(t) &= 4(1) - \frac{1}{2}(2t) \\ v_A(t) &= 4 - t \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Set up the speed equation
steps.content:
Speed is the magnitude (absolute value) of velocity. The question asks when the speeds are equal.

*   **Speed of A:** $|v_A(t)| = |4 - t|$
*   **Speed of B:** The velocity is given as constant $2 \text{ m s}^{-1}$, so the speed is $|2| = 2$.

Equating the speeds gives us the modulus equation:

$$|4 - t| = 2$$

steps.title: Solve Case 1 (Positive)
steps.content:
To solve the absolute value equation $|4-t|=2$, we consider two cases. 

**Case 1:** The expression inside the absolute value is equal to positive 2.

$$\begin{aligned} 4 - t &= 2 \\ 4 - 2 &= t \\ t &= 2 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 4 - 2

ti84.instructions: Calculate 4 - 2

desmos.instructions: Calculate 4 - 2

calculator.description: Verify calculation for Case 1

steps.title: Solve Case 2 (Negative)
steps.content:
Now we consider the second case where the expression inside the absolute value equals the negative value.

**Case 2:** The expression inside the absolute value is equal to -2.

$$\begin{aligned} 4 - t &= -2 \\ 4 + 2 &= t \\ t &= 6 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 4 + 2

ti84.instructions: Calculate 4 + 2

desmos.instructions: Calculate 4 + 2

calculator.description: Verify calculation for Case 2

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Both solutions are valid since $t \geq 0$. Note that at $t=2$, velocity is $2 \text{ m s}^{-1}$, and at $t=6$, velocity is $-2 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. Both have a speed of $2 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.

The speeds are equal at:

$$t = 2 \text{ s} \quad \text{and} \quad t = 6 \text{ s}$$

steps.title: Find the velocity of Particle A
steps.content:
To find the speed, we first need the velocity expression for Particle A. Velocity is the derivative of displacement with respect to time ($v = \frac{ds}{dt}$). 

Given the displacement $s_{A}(t) = 4t - \frac{1}{2}t^2$, apply the power rule for differentiation:

$$v_A(t) = \frac{d}{dt}\left(4t - \frac{1}{2}t^2\right) = 4 - t$$

steps.title: Identify speed functions
steps.content:
Speed is the magnitude (absolute value) of velocity. We need to define the speed functions for both particles.

*   **Particle A:** The speed is $|v_A(t)| = |4 - t|$.
*   **Particle B:** The velocity is constant at $2 \text{ m s}^{-1}$, so the speed is simply $2$.

We need to find $t$ where these speeds are equal:
$$|4 - t| = 2$$

steps.title: Graph the functions
steps.content:
To solve this graphically, graph the speed of Particle A as $y_1$ and the speed of Particle B as $y_2$ on your calculator. 

Note: Use $x$ as the variable for time $t$ when entering the functions.

1. Go to Graph mode.
2. Enter y1 = |4-x|.
3. Enter y2 = 2.
4. Draw the graph.

2. Enter Y1 = abs(4-X). (Press [Math] > NUM > 1:abs( ).)
3. Enter Y2 = 2.
4. Press [Graph]. (Adjust [Window] if needed: Xmin=0, Xmax=10, Ymin=0, Ymax=5).

desmos.instructions:
1. Enter y = |4-x| in the first line.
2. Enter y = 2 in the second line.

calculator.description: Plot the speed functions to find their intersection points.

steps.title: Find intersection points
steps.content: Use the intersection tool on your calculator to find the $x$-coordinates (which represent time $t$) where the two graphs cross.

gdc.instructions:
1. Select G-Solv (or equivalent).
2. Select ISCT (Intersection).
3. Note both x-values provided.
